coredata.dc:title: A novel label-free electrochemical immunosensor based on functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for carcinoembryonic antigen detection
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yuying
Liu, Qing
Liu, Yan
Cui, Jianjian
Liu, Hui
Wang, Ping
Li, Yueyun
Chen, Lei
Zhao, Zengdian
Dong, Yunhui
coredata.dc:description: A novel and ultrasensitive label-free electrochemical immunosensor was fabricated for quantitative detection of carcino-embryonic antigen (CEA). The nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) supported PtPd bimetallic nanoparticles (PtPd/N-GQDs) were synthesized by a simple and green hydrothermal procedure. Subsequently, PtPd/N-GQDs functionalized Au nanoparticles (PtPd/N-GQDs@Au) were prepared successfully via a self-assembly approach. Because of the synergetic effect present in PtPd/N-GQDs@Au, this novel nanocomposites has shown excellent electrocatalytic activity towards hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) reduction. Featuring good biocompatibility, excellent conductivity and large surface area, PtPd/N-GQDs@Au was applied as transducing materials to efficiently conjugate capture antibodies and amplify electrochemical signal. Under the optimal conditions, the proposed immunosensor was used for the detection of CEA with wide dynamic range in the range from 5 fg/mL to 50ng/mL with a low detection limit of 2fg/mL (S/N=3). Furthermore, this label-free immunosensor possesses high sensitivity, special selectivity and long-term stability, which shows promising application in bioassay analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carcinoembryonic antigen
Au nanoparticles
PtPd bimetallic nanoparticles
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Label-free
Electrochemical immunosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N-GQDs-supported PtPd Bimetallic Nanoparticles (PtPd/N-GQDs)
Preparation of N-GQDs-supported PtPd NPs loaded on Au Nanoparticles (PtPd/N-GQDs@Au)
The mechanism of multiple signal amplification

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of the label-free electrochemical immunosensor and the preparation procedure of PtPd/N-GQDs@Au.
TEM image: (A) N-GQDs, (B, C) PtPd/N-GQDs, (D) Au NPs, (E, F) PtPd/N-GQDs@Au. EDX spectrum: (G) PtPd/N-GQDs; (H) PtPd/N-GQDs@Au.

(A) Ameperometic response: (a) bare GCE, (b) N-GQDs, (c) Au NPs, (d) PtPd/N-GQDs, (e) PtPd/N-GQDs@Au. Nyquist plots of the EIS (B) and CV (C) method: GCE (a), PtPd/N-GQDs@Au/GCE (b), anti-CEA/PtPd/N-GQDs@Au/GCE (c), BSA/anti-CEA/PtPd/N-GQDs@Au/GCE (d), CEA/BSA/anti-CEA/PtPd/N-GQDs@Au/GCE (e).
The optimization of experimental conditions of pH (A), the PtPd/N-GQDs@Au concentration (B), Error bar= RSD (n=5).

(A) Amperometric response of the immunosensor to different concentration of CEA, from a to h: 5 fg/mL, 50 fg/mL, 500 fg/mL, 5pg/mL, 50pg/mL, 500pg/mL, 5ng/mL, 50ng/mL; (B) Calibration curves of immunosensor to different concentrations of CEA, error bar=RSD (n=5).

(A) Amperometric change response of biosensor to different electrodes treated in same way. 1–5, 1ng/mL. 6–10, 5fg/mL; (B) Current responses of the immunosensor to 50ng/mL HBS (1), 50ng/mL PSA (2), 50ng/mL IgG (3), 50ng/mL BSA (4), 0.5ng/mL CEA+50ng/mL HBS (5), 0.5ng/mL CEA+50ng/mL PSA(6), 0.5ng/mL CEA+50ng/mL IgG (7), 0.5ng/mL CEA+50ng/mL BSA(8). (C) The stability study of the CEA immunosensor. Error bar=RSD (n =5).

head.ce:title: A novel label-free electrochemical immunosensor based on functionalized nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for carcinoembryonic antigen detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo 255049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering, Shandong University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analysis and Testing Center, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo 255049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Analysis and Testing Center, Shandong University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel and ultrasensitive label-free electrochemical immunosensor was fabricated for quantitative detection of carcino-embryonic antigen (CEA). The nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) supported PtPd bimetallic nanoparticles (PtPd/N-GQDs) were synthesized by a simple and green hydrothermal procedure. Subsequently, PtPd/N-GQDs functionalized Au nanoparticles (PtPd/N-GQDs@Au) were prepared successfully via a self-assembly approach. Because of the synergetic effect present in PtPd/N-GQDs@Au, this novel nanocomposites has shown excellent electrocatalytic activity towards hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) reduction. Featuring good biocompatibility, excellent conductivity and large surface area, PtPd/N-GQDs@Au was applied as transducing materials to efficiently conjugate capture antibodies and amplify electrochemical signal. Under the optimal conditions, the proposed immunosensor was used for the detection of CEA with wide dynamic range in the range from 5 fg/mL to 50ng/mL with a low detection limit of 2fg/mL (S/N=3). Furthermore, this label-free immunosensor possesses high sensitivity, special selectivity and long-term stability, which shows promising application in bioassay analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Carcino-embryonic antigen (CEA), a highly glycosylated protein with molecular weight of approximately 200kDa, is one of the most broad spectrum biological marker, and it is elevated in many malignancies, such as gastric cancer, colorectal cancer, breast cancer, liver cancer and pancreatic cancer (Thomas et al., 1995). In addition, the levels of CEA are extremely low in the colon tissue of adults at the range between 2.5 and 5.0μg/L, but greatly elevated in patients with liver cancer (Zamcheck and Martin, 1981). The assay for CEA is commonly used during the tracking of patients having undergone curative resection (Wang et al., 1994). Therefore, a highly sensitive, selective and quantitative determination method for CEA is great demanded.

A series of methods have been used for the detection of low-abundance CEA such as electrochemiluminescent (Deng et al., 2013), electrochemical (Wen et al., 2016), surface-enhanced Raman scattering (Chon et al., 2009), fluorescent (Wang et al., 2014), mass spectrometric (Liu et al., 2011), chemiluminescent (Zong et al., 2012), and surface plasmon resonance techniques (Špringer et al., 2013). In comparisons, electrochemical immunosensor has gained wide attentions because of its economical, sensitive, portable, easy-to-operate, and simple-to-construct properties (Ahirwal and Mitra, 2010; Zhao et al., 2014). As a significant branch of electrochemical immunosensors, the label-free immunosensors have an advantage over sandwich-type immunosensors because the sensor architecture is simpler (Joung et al., 2013; Li et al., 2016). Furthermore, the nonenzymatic label-free immunosensors have overcome some obstacles of enzyme-based biosensors, such as environmental instability, tedious immobilization procedures, and high cost (Gopalan et al., 2013).

In order to amplify the detection signal, various types of materials is used to fabricate the immunosensors, including nanoparticles (Han et al., 2016), quantum dots (Martín-Yerga et al., 2014), metal oxides (Ansari et al., 2008) and electroactive component-encapsulated nanoparticles (Viswanathan et al., 2009). Among the nanoparticles, the noble metal with well-defined, low-cost and controlled shape have attracted increasing attention and become a kind of promising materials in ultrasensitive chemical and biological molecules detections. For example, metal nanocatalysts, gold nanoparticles, platinum nanoparticles and palladium nanoparticles have been widely applied for the fabrication of electrochemical biosensors due to their well biocompatibility, excellent electrocatalytic activity, and high electrode conductivity (El-Deab and Ohsaka, 2002; Han et al., 2016; Roushani and Valipour, 2016). Compared with single metal nanoparticles, bimetallic nanoparticles combine the catalytic effect of their monometallic counterparts to generate distinctly unique characteristics (Cao et al., 2013). It is well-known that, in electrocatalysis and electrochemical immunosensor applications, metal nanoparticles are usually dispersed on supporting materials to obtain high conductivity, stability and large surface (Rajabzade et al., 2012). Because of their excellent properties, carbon materials have been widely used as catalyst supports, such as carbon nanotubes, ordered mesoporous carbon, graphene etc (Tang et al., 2011; Yang et al., 2016). Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been applied as a robust substrate in electrocatalytic determination due to the advantages of large specific surface area, biocompatibility, low toxicity, excellent electrical conductivity and good thermal conductivity (Ju and Chen, 2015). According to the previous research, if doping heteroatoms into the π-conjugated system, the structure defects of GQDs may be manipulated (Li et al., 2011). For the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), the chemically bonded N atoms could drastically produce new phenomena and unexpected properties, for example, altering the electronic characteristics and increasing the number of anchoring sites for the adsorption of metal ions (Chizari et al., 2010; Li et al., 2011). At the same time, N-GQDs possess many oxygen-containing functional groups that can make it better water-solubility and easier to form a stable chemical bond with various materials. Therefore, N-GQDs supported bimetallic nanoparticles could have good biocompatibility and superior auxiliary catalytic activity. Herein, PtPd bimetallic nanoparticles (PtPd NPs) were supported on N-GQDs by hydrothermal coreduction of platinum and palladium using N-GQDs as both reducing agents and supports. The intimate electronic interactions between N-GQDs and PtPd NPs provided naked catalytic surface area and high electrical conductivity, which rendered them the highly active electrocatalysts for H2O2 reduction.

For the development of highly sensitive electrochemical immunosensor, Au nanoparticles (Au NPs) were considered as the most stable biocompatible nano-scaled materials (Mani et al., 2009; Zhuo et al., 2005). In this system, Au NPs, about 30nm in diameter, were used to amplify electrocatalytic activity and link antibodies. N-GQDs supported PtPd NPs (PtPd/N-GQDs) were then functionalized by amino groups and finally self-assembly loaded on Au NPs according to the covalent bonding. Attributed to the effective immobilization of antibodies and the synergistic catalytic activity of PtPd NPs, Au NPs and N-GQDs towards the reduction of H2O2, the as-prepared PtPd/N-GQDs@Au nanocomposites were applied as a signal amplification platform to fabricate a novel and ultrasensitive label-free immunosensor for the quantitative detection of CEA. The proposed novel immunosensor strategy showed high sensitivity and good stability for quantitative determination of CEA in human serum, which held a great potential in clinical and diagnostic applications.

The mixed Na2HPO4 and KH2PO4 stock solution were prepared to form phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH=6.47) that used as the electrolyte in the process of electrochemical measurements. Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA, 96–99%) was purchased from Sigma reagent Co., Ltd. (St. Louis, MO, USA). Citric acid (CA) and dicyandiamide (DCD) were purchased from Bodi Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). PdCl2 and PtCl2 were bought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. K3[Fe(CN)6] was bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). CEA and CEA antibody (anti-CEA) were purchased from Linc-Bio Science Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All the experimental processes used ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ cm, 24°C). All solvents were of analytical grade. The prepared solutions were maintained at 4°C prior to use.

Electrochemical measurements were recorded using a CHI760E electrochemical workstation by using a conventional three-electrode system consisted of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as working electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, and the platinum wire electrode as the counter electrode (Chenhua Instrument Shanghai Co., Ltd, China). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analysis were collected using a Tecnai G2 F20 (America).

N-GQDs were prepared by following a literature procedure (Ju and Chen, 2015). Briefly, 2.0g of CA and 1.0g of DCD were mixed with ultrapure water (5mL). Then, the mixture was transferred into a 25mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated for 12h at 180°C. Eventually, the product was dispersed in 100mL of ultrapure water, and the N-GQDs were collected by removing the large dots through centrifugation at 10,000rpm for 10min

According to the classical method, seed Au NPs were prepared using sodium citrate to reduce AuCl4 − Ions (Frens, 1973). In brief, HAuCl4 (0.01wt%, 100mL) was refluxed and heated to boiling. Then sodium citrate (2.5mL, 10mg/mL−1) was rapidly added into the solution and stirred for another 15min. The resultant wine-red solution was cooled down to room temperature and stored at 4°C for further usage.

These seed Au NPs were then used for the synthesis of Au NPs with an average diameter of 50nm by the following procedure (Brown et al., 2000). The prepared seed Au NPs (1mL) and NH2OH·HCl (100μL，0.2M) were mixed to ultrapure water (25mL) and was stirred vigorously at room temperature. By adding HAuCl4 (3.0mL, 0.1wt%) drop-wise, a crimson solution was obtained and stored at 4°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-GQDs-supported PtPd Bimetallic Nanoparticles (PtPd/N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: PtPd/N-GQDs were synthesized through a simple hydrothermal method (He et al., 2013; Peng et al., 2012; Song and Chen, 2014). Typically, PtCl2 (6.65mg, 0.025mmol) and PdCl2 (5.75mg, 0.025mmol) were dissolved in 1mL of hydrochloric acid under heating. The solution was then added into a 15mL aqueous solution with 20mg of N-GQDs. The pH was adjusted to 10 with NaOH (0.1mg/mL), and the mixture was transferred to Teflon-lined autoclave (160 kPa) and hydrothermally treated at 160°C for 6h. Black precipitates appeared in the flask as a result of thermolytic reduction of Pt，Pd on N-GQDs to form PtPd/N-GQDs NPs. The precipitates were collected, washed extensively with Nanopure water and ethanol, and dried in a vacuum oven at room temperature for 12h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-GQDs-supported PtPd NPs loaded on Au Nanoparticles (PtPd/N-GQDs@Au)
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain the amino-functionalized PtPd/N-GQDs, 1.0mL PtPd/N-GQDs and 2.0mL ethylenediamine was first dispersed in 5mL ultrapure water by sonication (240W) for 1h. After stirring for 5h, the suspension was centrifuged and washed with ultrapure water for three times, repeatedly. For further functionalization, the resulting amino-functionalized PtPd/N-GQDs was dispersed in 1.0mL as-prepared Au NPs and the mixed suspension was stirred for 12h. After immobilizing PtPd/N-GQDs on the surface of Au NPs, the resultant product was thoroughly rinsed with water. The sediment was dried and the obtained powder was designated as PtPd/N-GQDs@Au.

The schematic diagram of the stepwise self-assembly procedure of the proposed label-free immunosensor is shown in Fig. 1. Briefly, the glassy carbon electrode (GCE, Φ=4mm) was carefully polished with 1.0 and 0.3µm alumina powder separately prior to fabrication of the immunosensor, and rinsed thoroughly with ultrapure water between each polishing step. The polished GCE was first coated with PtPd/N-GQDs@Au nanocomposite (3.5mg/mL, 6μL) and dried in air. After drying for 1h, the resultant PtPd/N-GQDs@Au/GCE was incubated with anti-CEA (10μg/mL, 6μL) by the chemical bonding between PtPd NPs and available amine groups of anti-CEA. After the step of drying and washing, BSA solution (1wt%, 3μL) was used to eliminate nonspecific binding sites between the antigen and the electrode surface. After 1h incubation, the BSA/anti-CEA/PtPd/N-GQDs@Au/GCE was washed with ultrapure water and incubated with different concentrations of CEA (5 fg/mL to 50ng/mL, 6mL) for 1h at room temperature, and then the CEA/BSA/anti-CEA/PtPd/N-GQDs@Au/GCE was washed extensively to remove unbound CEA molecules. Ultimately, the proposed immunosensor was stored at 4°C for further usage.

The modification process of the electrodes was characterized by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) experiments. The CVs (100mV/s) were recorded in 5.0mMK3[Fe(CN)6] by scanning the potential from −1.0V to 1.0V and the EIS Nyquist plots were recorded from 1 to 105 Hz in a solution containing 0.1mol/L KCl and 2.5mmol/L Fe(CN)6 3−/4−. Amperometric i–t curve was used for CEA determination by scanning the potential at −0.4V using PBS (0.1M, pH 6.47) as the electrolyte. To record the amperometric response, 5.0mM (10μL) H2O2 was added into the PBS (10mL) after the current was stabilized under stirring.

Fig. 2A showed the TEM image of N-GQDs, the average size of N-GQDs was 10nm in diameter and their size distribution was relatively uniform in a low-contrast background. As shown in Fig. 2B and C, an overview TEM image displayed relatively uniform morphology of PtPd/N-GQDs with an average diameter of 15nm. The synthesized nanoparticles were stable, uniform and well dispersed in water, which indicated PtPd NPs were successfully and efficiently loaded on N-GQDs with well monodispersion and uniformly spherical shape. Obviously, Pt and Pd elements were observed in the EDX of PtPd/N-GQDs, which further demonstrated the PtPd was attached on the surface of N-GQDs (Fig. 2G). When the PtPd/N-GQDs NPs were self-assembly loaded on the surface of Au NPs (Fig. 2D), the surface morphology was greatly alternated (Fig. 2E and F). EDX spectrum of PtPd/N-GQDs@Au nanocomposites clearly confirmed the presence of PtPd/N-GQDs NPs attached on the surface of the Au NPs (Fig. 2H). SEM images and FT-IR spectrum further demonstrated that the PtPd/N-GQDs NPs were self-assembly loaded on Au NPs (Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of multiple signal amplification
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of the label-free immunosensor was affected directly by the superior electrocatalytic properties of PtPd/N-GQDs@Au towards the reduction of 5.0mMH2O2. For comparative analysis, the amperometric i-t curve was recorded at −0.4V in PBS at pH=6.47 to assess the catalytic properties of bare GCE, N-GQDs, Au NPs, PtPd/N-GQDs, PtPd/N-GQDs@Au to H2O2. As can be seen from Fig. 3A, the bare GCE did not show catalytic effect towards the reduction of H2O2 (curve a) and the signal increased slightly when N-GQDs were loaded on the electrode (curve b). A somewhat larger current response was observed when Au NPs was applied to modify the bare GCE (curve c). Spontaneously, when PtPd/N-GQDs NPs were loaded on the bare GCE, the current response was significantly increased. The enhanced electrocatalytic property of PtPd/N-GQDs modified GCE could be ascribed to the following possible aspects. Firstly, the alloyed PtPd NPs provide plenty of adsorption sites for −OH groups of H2O2. Secondly, the surfactant-free PtPd NPs provide a naked catalytic surface that promotes the electrocatalytic activity. Thirdly, the intimate electronic interactions between N-GQDs and PtPd NPs can improve the electron transfer. As expected, when PtPd/N-GQDs@Au NPs were used as signal amplification platform, the current response was the highest, which could be attributed to that the Au NPs not only acted as the matrix for PtPd/N-GQDs fictionalization to provide more active sites, but also participated in the electrocatalytic reaction. The synergistic effect of Au NPs and PtPd/N-GQDs NPs contributed to the enhanced electrochemical response of the PtPd/N-GQDs@Au modified GCE for the reduction of H2O2. Hence, PtPd/N-GQDs@Au nanocomposites were adopted to act as signal amplification platform to fabricate a label-free immunosensor with high sensitivity.

EIS and CV were used to monitor the immobilization steps to evidence the successful fabrication of proposed immunosensor. As reported, EIS is a well-established technique in studying the feature of the surface-modified electrodes (Eckhard et al., 2007; Macdonald, 1987). Therefore, EIS was used to monitor the interfacial properties and the changes of the electron-transfer resistance (Ret). The Nyquist diagrams of EIS consisted of two parts: a semicircle at high frequencies and a linear at low frequencies. The semicircle is associated with the electron transfer Ret corresponding to the electron-transfer-limited process and the linear portion corresponds to that of the diffusion (Cui et al., 2008; Santaclara et al., 2014). As shown in Fig. 3B, the bare GCE exhibited a smaller Ret (curve a). Owing to the high electrical transport properties of PtPd/N-GQDs@Au, the semicircle diameter was decreased when the PtPd/N-GQDs@Au was dropped onto the surface of electrode (curve b). After incubation with anti-CEA, the Ret was significantly increased (curve c), implying that anti-CEA was immobilized on the electrode successfully. The Ret was further increased due to the modification of protein molecules of BSA on the surface of the electrode, which can block the nonspecific binding spots (curve d). Additionally, the Ret further increased with the addition of CEA (curve e). As a result, we can conclude that the proposed immunosensor was fabricated successfully.

For probing the interface properties of surface-modified electrodes, CVs were also used for recording each immobilization step at a scan rate of 100mV/s (Fig. 3C). When the PtPd/N-GQDs@Au covered the surface of electrode, the current increased. However, the current decreased gradually when the anti-CEA, BSA and CEA were added onto the surface of electrode respectively, which indicated that the immunosensor was fabricated successfully. Both the EIS and CV results illustrated that the PtPd/N-GQDs@Au accelerates the electron transfer and the anti-CEA, BSA and CEA hinder the electrons transfer to the electrode surface.
The experimental conditions including pH and the concentration of PtPd/N-GQDs@Au were optimized to obtain a better electrocatalytic signal.

The pH of the PBS was investigated under the same concentration of PtPd/N-GQDs@Au (3.5mg/mL). As shown in Fig. 4A, the current signal increased with the variation of pH from 5.12 to 6.47 and then decreased with the variation of pH from 6.47 to 8.04, which inferred that the pH value obviously affected the electrocatalytic process of PtPd/N-GQDs@Au toward the reduction of H2O2. Simultaneously, the highly acidic or alkaline environment would influence the activity of protein, for example, antigens and antibodies (Han et al., 2013; Katz and Willner, 2005). Therefore, pH of 6.47 was selected to achieve the optimal amperometric response.

The concentration of PtPd/N-GQDs@Au has a significant influence on the amperometric response of the immunosensor. Fig. 4B showed the different amperometric response of the electrode modified with different concentration of PtPd/N-GQDs@Au nanocomposites in PBS at pH of 6.47. The current signal enhanced with the increasing of the concentrations from 1.0mg/mL to 3.5mg/mL but decreased with the concentrations from 3.5mg/mL to 5.0mg/mL. The optimal amperometric response was obtained at 3.5mg/mL. These results indicated that the PtPd/N-GQDs@Au could increase the electroconductibility and contacting area for electron transfer and antibodies immobilization. But in high concentration, the interface electron transfer resistance may be increased (Jiang et al., 2015). Therefore, the concentration of 3.5mg/mL was selected as the optimal concentration for the test.

Under the optimal conditions, the performance study of the proposed immunosensor was carried out by amperometric i–t at −0.4V in pH 6.47 PBS to detect different concentrations of CEA using PtPd/N-GQDs@Au as a signal amplification platform towards the H2O2 reduction ( Fig. 5). The detection of CEA was based on the degree of inhibited electron transfer of immobilized PtPd/N-GQDs@Au, which was hindered by the formation of antigen-antibody complex on the PtPd/N-GQDs@Au modified GCE. As shown in Fig. 5A, the signal response of the immunosensor decreased along with the increasing CEA concentration in the incubation solution. Fig. 5B described the current response was linearly related to the logarithmic values of the CEA concentration with the range from 5 fg/mL to 50ng/mL and an extremely low limit of detection wss 2fg/mL (S/N=3). The regression equation of the calibration curve was: I=105.3−9.873 logC/2 with correlation coefficient of 0.997. The results indicated that the proposed method had an acceptable quantitative performance for the CEA detection.

The performance of the proposed immunosensor was compared with previously reported methods for the detection of CEA and the result was showed in Table S1. The proposed sensitive immunosensor had a wider linear range and lower detection limit than previously reports, which may be attributed to the following factors. Firstly, PtPd/N-GQDs@Au, a novel nanomaterial combined with Au NPs and PtPd/N-GQDs NPs through covalent bonding, possessed high electrocatalytic activity towards the reduction of H2O2 leading to higher sensitivity. Secondly, the remarkable electroconductibility of PtPd/N-GQDs@Au increased the electron transfer speed.

To evaluate the reproducibility of the immunosensor, two series of five different electrodes were prepared for the detection of 1ng/mL and 5 fg/mL of CEA ( Fig. 6A). The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the measurements for the ten electrodes were 2.6% and 2.1%, suggesting the reproducibility of the immunosensor was acceptable.

Investigating the specificity of the electrochemical immunosensor was also extremely important (Fig. 6B) (Han et al., 2016). Interference study was performed by using hepatitis B surface antigen (HBS), prostate specific antigen (PSA), human immunoglobulin (IgG) and BSA. 50ng/mL interfering substances with 0.5ng/mL CEA solution was measured by the immunosensor. The variation of amperometric responsen of samples with interference and CEA was less than 5% of that without interferences, indicating the selectivity of the designed immunosensor was acceptable.

The stability of the prepared immunosensor was inspected by keeping the electrodes at 4°C with the current response being tested per 4 days. As shown in Fig. 6C, the current response of the immunosensor kept constant after 4 days. After 8 days, the current response of the immunosensor only had a minor change of 2.9%. The current response retained 92.3% and 86.5% of their initial current after 12 days and 16 days respectively. The good stability of the designed immunosensor can be ascribed to the good biocompatibility of the Au NPs and PtPd nanocomposites.
The reproducibility, selectivity and stability of the designed immunosensor were all acceptable, thus it was suitable for quantitative detection of CEA in real human samples.

In order to validate accuracy and the feasibility of the proposed immunoassay for real sample analysis, standard addition method was used to detect the recoveries of different concentrations of CEA in the serum sample (Table S2). The concentrations of 1, 5 and 10ng/mL CEA solutions were added into serum samples. The RSD was ranged from 3.507% to 6.229% and the recovery rate was from 100.0% to 108.1%. The results imply that the proposed immunosensor could be effectively applied in the clinical determination of the CEA levels in serum samples.

A novel and ultrasensitive label-free electrochemical immunosensor has been developed for the quantitative detection of CEA using PtPd/N-GQDs@Au as a signal amplification platform. To design a high performance electrochemical immunosensor, PtPd/N-GQDs@Au was modified on the electrode, which could effectively capture antibodies and amplify the detection signal. The proposed label-free immunosensor displayed wide linear range, low detection limit, high sensitivity, good reproducibility, long-term stability and acceptable selectivity. Herein, this proposed method will not only expand the application of the new nanocomposite, but also provide an attractive way to detect other biomolecules in clinical diagnosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We greatly appreciate the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21302057; 21405095; 21575079), the Young Talents Joint Fund of Shandong Province (ZR2015JL005), CPSF (2016M590736) and Special Funding for Postdoctoral Innovation Project of Shandong Province (201501002).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2016.11.029.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
